name: Salesforce
researcher_notes:
Salesforce (salesforce.com / developer.salesforce.com) buildability as an AI-agent toolkit (MCP server or agent-callable actions) is **strong** because Salesforce exposes a large API surface that supports OAuth-based authorization and fine-grained security. There are also **public MCP server implementations** already.

## 1) Auth methods used by the API
Salesforce REST API access requires OAuth 2.0 via “external client apps or connected apps” (OAuth 2.0 authorization flow).  
- Auth method: **OAuth 2.0 using connected apps / external client apps** (token-based requests).  
  URL: https://developer.salesforce.com/docs/atlas.en-us.api_rest.meta/api_rest/intro_oauth_and_connected_apps.htm

Salesforce REST quickstart notes you must obtain an **access token** to send requests.  
- URL: https://developer.salesforce.com/docs/atlas.en-us.api_rest.meta/api_rest/quickstart_oauth.htm

Salesforce also explicitly documents that the authorization outcome includes **access and refresh tokens** in OAuth flows (describing the token-based model).  
- URL: https://developer.salesforce.com/docs/atlas.en-us.chatterapi.meta/chatterapi/intro_using_oauth.htm

## 2) Self-serve vs gated credentials access (quote the decisive phrase)
Salesforce provides a **free Developer Edition** environment that developers can sign up for themselves.

“Dev orgs are a free way to get started building apps in Salesforce… **Anyone can sign up for a Developer Edition** and create an org.”  
- URL: https://trailhead.salesforce.com/content/learn/modules/environments-for-developers/explore-available-developer-environments

The Developer Edition signup form is hosted on developer.salesforce.com and describes the environment as free/non-production. The signup page content includes:
- “**Get a Development Environment for Free**”
- “**Developer Edition** … Build on Salesforce **for free**”
- and explicitly shows “**https://developer.salesforce.com/signup Get a Developer Edition**”
- URL: https://developer.salesforce.

name: HubSpot
researcher_notes:
HubSpot (hubspot.com) agent-toolkit buildability research (evidence-backed)

1) Auth method(s) the HubSpot API uses
- OAuth access tokens (Bearer): HubSpot’s app-level OAuth tokens authenticate API calls via the HTTP header `Authorization: Bearer <token>` (example curl shown) and are obtained via OAuth flows.  
  Source: https://developers.hubspot.com/docs/apps/developer-platform/build-apps/authentication/overview
- Static auth access tokens (Bearer): For installing into a single account, HubSpot provides a static auth access token used the same way: `Authorization: Bearer <token>`.  
  Source: https://developers.hubspot.com/docs/apps/developer-platform/build-apps/authentication/overview
- Developer API keys (hapikey query param): Some app features/settings require a “developer API key”, provided via the `hapikey` query parameter (often with `appId`).  
  Source: https://developers.hubspot.com/docs/apps/developer-platform/build-apps/authentication/overview
- Client credentials tokens (OAuth 2.0 token endpoint; Bearer-style usage): HubSpot describes “client credential tokens” for specific functionality (webhooks journal API).  
  Source: https://developers.hubspot.com/docs/apps/developer-platform/build-apps/authentication/overview
- Additional note on service keys (BETA): HubSpot also documents “service keys” that can be used as a Bearer token for REST API requests.  
  Source: https://developers.hubspot.com/docs/apps/developer-platform/build-apps/authentication/account-service-keys

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access (actually verifying “free/trial vs paid/admin approval/contact sales”)
- Free self-serve developer account + developer test accounts:
  - “Do I need a paid HubSpot account to develop? No. You can create a free HubSpot account and build apps without a paid subscription.”  
  - “You'll get access to a developer test account with sample data for testing.”  
  Sources:  
  https://developers.hubspot.com/developer-platform-basics
- 90-day trial for dev

name: Attio
researcher_notes:
Attio (attio.com) appears **agent-toolkit buildable today**, and in fact it already has an **official hosted MCP server** you can point agents at. Below is evidence-backed research for auth, credential access, API surface, MCP existence, and buildability.

---

## 1) Auth method(s) the Attio API uses
From Attio’s REST API authentication docs:

- Attio REST API supports **OAuth 2.0 access tokens** and **single-workspace API keys**:
  - “In order to make requests to the Attio REST API, you need to generate an access token. There are two ways to generate an access token: 1. By implementing an OAuth 2.0 flow 2. By generating an API key for your workspace …”  
  URL: https://docs.attio.com/rest-api/guides/authentication
- Both token types are sent as a bearer token:
  - “Pass the value of the token in the `Authorization` header of your requests like so.”  
  - “Authorization: Bearer <access_token>”  
  URL: https://docs.attio.com/rest-api/guides/authentication
- It also mentions **HTTP Basic**, where the username is the token (blank password), but recommends Bearer:
  - “We also support HTTP Basic Authentication, where the username is the token and the password is left blank. However, we recommend using Bearer authentication …”  
  URL: https://docs.attio.com/rest-api/guides/authentication

MCP auth is also OAuth-based (see MCP section below).

---

## 2) Self-serve vs gated credentials (API keys / OAuth client creds)
### What Attio says about generating tokens
Attio documents that you can generate an API key in the developer settings page for single-workspace use:
- “If you only need a token for a single workspace, you can generate an API key in the developer settings page of your apps.”  
  URL: https://docs.attio.com/rest-api/guides/authentication

And for multi-workspace, you’d use OAuth 2.0 (implying you configure an app and obtain OAuth credentials in the developer dashboard):
- “You should prefer the OAuth 2.0 flow if building an app for multiple workspaces.”

name: Copper
researcher_notes:
Caveat: “Copper” appears to have **two different Copper API/documentation systems**:
1) **Copper Developer API (“Dev API”)** for the CRM, hosted at `developer.copper.com` and called via `https://api.copper.com/developer_api/v1/...`
2) **Copper Platform API** (different product surface), hosted at `developer.copper.co` and called via `https://api.copper.co/platform/...`  
Below I focus on the **CRM Dev API** (agent toolkit relevance), but I also note auth for the platform API where it’s clearly documented.

---

1) Auth method(s) used
**Copper Developer API (CRM Dev API)**
- Copper documents **two auth methods**: **API keys** and **OAuth2.0**:  
  https://developer.copper.com/introduction/authentication.html
- **API keys**: “The Dev API uses token based authentication… You have to include the token in the header of every request, along with the email address of the user who generated the token.”  
  https://developer.copper.com/introduction/authentication.html
- **OAuth2.0**: uses the **Authorization Code Grant** flow and returns `token_type: "Bearer"` access tokens, which are then included in an `Authorization: Bearer {access_token}` header for Dev API calls:  
  https://developer.copper.com/introduction/oauth/flow.html

**Copper Platform API (non-CRM surface; included because it’s also on Copper’s developer domain)**
- Auth is via `Authorization` header with an API key plus request signing headers `X-Signature` and `X-Timestamp`:  
  https://developer.copper.co/api-reference/authentication

---

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access
**Self-serve availability (trial + ability to obtain credentials)**
- Copper’s signup page explicitly offers: **“Start your free trial - Free 14-day trial - No credit card required”**  
  https://www.copper.com/signup
- Copper’s Dev API docs explain how to generate API credentials:  
  “To generate an API token, in the Copper web app go to System settings > API Keys and click the 'GENERATE API KEY' button.”  
  https://developer.copp

name: Zendesk
researcher_notes:
1) Auth method(s) the Zendesk API uses  
- Zendesk supports **API token authentication** using **HTTP Basic auth** with `{email_address}/token:{api_token}` encoded and sent in the `Authorization` header. Source: https://developer.zendesk.com/api-reference/introduction/security-and-auth/  
- Zendesk supports **OAuth access token authentication** using `Authorization: Bearer {access_token}`. Source: https://developer.zendesk.com/api-reference/introduction/security-and-auth/  
- Zendesk also notes that if you’re distributing an app/integration/bot to multiple Zendesk customers, you **must use global OAuth access tokens** (and “may not use regular API tokens or OAuth tokens” for that use case). Source: https://developer.zendesk.com/api-reference/introduction/security-and-auth/  

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access (trials, signup, admin approval)  
- Zendesk has a **self-serve free trial signup**: the registration page says **“Start your free 14-day trial”** and **“100% free. No credit card required.”** Source: https://www.zendesk.com/register/  
- To use **API tokens**, Zendesk states you must enable token access in the admin settings: **“You use API tokens… API token access is disabled by default. You must enable API token access before users can use API tokens.”** Source: https://support.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/articles/8662004958746-Turning-on-and-off-API-access  
- Zendesk further states **“To generate an API token, you must be an administrator and API token access must be turned on in your account.”** Source: https://support.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/articles/4408889192858-Managing-API-token-access-to-the-Zendesk-API  
- For **OAuth access tokens**, the docs describe that creating OAuth tokens typically involves an OAuth flow requiring user interaction (and for token creation via API: **“Only admins can make the request.”**). Source: https://developer.zendesk.com/documentation/api-basics/authentication/api-tokens-to-oauth/ and https://developer.zendesk.com/documenta

name: Pumble
researcher_notes:
Pumble (pumble.com) buildability as an **AI-agent toolkit / MCP server** looks **very feasible**, largely because Pumble already provides both (a) an official MCP server endpoint and (b) a fairly complete OAuth2-based app/API integration model.

## 1) Auth method(s) the API uses
**OAuth2** is the primary auth model for Pumble app access:
- “**Pumble uses OAuth2 in order to allow your app to access the API.**” https://cake-com.github.io/pumble-node-sdk/authorization.html

The SDK docs also specify request headers used for API calls:

Pumble’s API base domain used for OAuth/token exchange is:
- `https://api-ga.pumble.com` (shown in the OAuth2 access curl example and in other REST endpoint examples) https://cake-com.github.io/pumble-node-sdk/authorization.html and https://dev-docs.marketplace.cake.com/pumble/build/manifest.html

Pumble has:
- A **Free plan** (“Free” listed in pricing) https://pumble.com/pricing  
- A **7-day free trial** (“**You can try out all features for free for 7 days, no credit card required.**”) https://pumble.com/pricing

Pumble documents **self-serve generation of an API key** via an in-product “API addon”, with no explicit mention of paid-only gating:

name: Vonage

1) Auth method(s) the API uses
- Vonage APIs (e.g., Voice/Messages/etc.) use **API Key + API Secret with HTTP Basic auth** in the `Authorization` header for many newer APIs:

  Source: https://developer.vonage.com/en/getting-started/concepts/authentication
- For **Verify V2 specifically**, Vonage documents both **Basic auth** and **JWT Bearer auth**, and notes Basic auth limitations (no callbacks/webhooks; no ACL support):

  - “Basic Auth … Callbacks/webhooks are not supported”  
  Source: https://developer.vonage.com/en/verify/concepts/authentication

  URL: https://developers.google.com/google-ads/api/rest/auth (see “The Google Ads API uses OAuth 2.0… Both OAuth 2.0 user authentication flow and service account flow are supported.”)

2) Self-serve vs gated credentials access (quote required)
- A developer token is required to make API calls, and it is obtained via Google’s API Center in your **Google Ads manager account**:  
  URL: https://developers.google.com/google-ads/api/docs/api-policy/developer-token (states you can obtain one by signing up in API Center of your manager account; developer token controls access level/environment)
- The developer token process includes a review/approval step and can start at restricted access (“Test Account Access”), then be upgraded after review:
  - “After you've completed the application process… `Pending Approval` status. Your developer token now has `Test Account Access` level.”

- For the legacy (older) REST API, there is also **OAuth 1.0a “one-legged” authentication** (this is not the recommended v3 flow). Source: https://woocommerce.github.io/woocommerce-rest-api-docs/v3.html?shell=

name: Amazon Selling Partner
researcher_notes:
Auth method(s)
- SP-API uses **OAuth 2.0 via Amazon Login with Amazon (LWA)** for (at least) public apps. The “Connect to the SP-API” doc states you request an LWA access token via `https://api.amazon.com/auth/o2/token` using `grant_type=refresh_token` (and `client_id` / `client_secret`). It also notes the returned token type is `bearer` and that the access token is sent as `x-amz-access-token` to SP-API operations.  
  URL: https://developer-docs.amazon.com/sp-api/docs/connecting-to-the-selling-partner-api
- For **private applications**, SP-API supports **self-authorization** (still generating LWA refresh tokens, which are later exchanged for access tokens).  
  URL: https://developer-docs.amazon.com/sp-api/docs/self-authorization
- For SP-API “operation calling,” the docs are REST-based and describe calling SP-API endpoints by constructing URIs and adding headers including `x-amz-access-token` plus `x-amz-date` and `user-agent`. (The docs excerpt I retrieved did not explicitly describe AWS SigV4 in the “connecting” page content shown, so I’m not asserting SigV4 from this run.)  
  URL: https://developer-docs.amazon.com/sp-api/docs/connecting-to-the-selling-partner-api

Self-serve vs gated credentials / ability to get creds for free
- The doc set distinguishes private vs public developer/app onboarding:
  - Private apps can be **self-authorized** (“Self authorization workflow… generate a refresh token for each authorized account”).  
    URL: https://developer-docs.amazon.com/sp-api/docs/self-authorization
  - Private vs public apps also affect *who authorizes* the application (you self-authorize private apps; selling partners authorize public apps via OAuth).  
    URL: https://developer-docs.amazon.com/sp-api/docs/authorizing-selling-partner-api-applications
- However, I did **not** find in the fetched authoritative pages any phrase like “free trial”, “no credit card”, or “free developer credentials”; instead, SP-API registration clearly involves **Amazon review/

name: ClickUp
researcher_notes:
1) **Auth method(s)**  
- The ClickUp API supports **personal API tokens** and **OAuth2**. “To use the ClickUp API, you must authenticate every request. For personal use, authenticate with your personal API token. For apps or integrations that other people use, use the OAuth flow…” (https://developer.clickup.com/docs/authentication)  
- **Personal tokens**: “Personal tokens begin with `pk_`… Add the token to the header: `Authorization: {personal_token}`.” (https://developer.clickup.com/docs/authentication)  
- **OAuth2**: OAuth uses the **authorization code** grant type. “Grant Type: `Authorization Code`” and OAuth access tokens are sent as `Authorization: Bearer {access_token}` (https://developer.clickup.com/docs/authentication)  
- The docs also state: “Include the token in the Authorization header of your requests.” (https://developer.clickup.com/docs/Getting%20Started)

2) **Self-serve vs gated credential access**  
- **Personal API tokens are self-serve** for individual/testing use: the docs say “Generate or regenerate a Personal API Token” and instruct you to “Log in to ClickUp… Settings… Apps… Under API Token, click Generate or Regenerate.” plus: “You’ll need to sign in to your ClickUp account in order to generate or regenerate a token.” (https://developer.clickup.com/docs/authentication)  
- ClickUp’s signup page explicitly markets a self-serve free tier: “Save one day every week… **Get started. It's FREE**” and “**Free Forever. No Credit Card.**” (https://clickup.com/signup)  
- **OAuth app creation is gated by role**: “Only Workspace owners or admins can create OAuth apps.” (https://developer.clickup.com/docs/authentication)  

> What this means for agent-toolkit buildability: you can build a toolkit that uses **personal tokens** immediately after self-serve sign-up; but for a multi-user app you must support the OAuth flow, and **creating the OAuth client requires owner/admin** access in each workspace.

- Plaid uses an OAuth scope value `mcp:dashboard` for connecting to the **Dashboard MCP server** (this is still handled via `/oauth/token` with the Plaid OAuth endpoint). URL: https://plaid.com/docs/resources/mcp/index.html.md

  “To gain access to the Plaid API, create an account on the Plaid Dashboard . Once you've completed the signup process and acknowledged our terms, we'll provide a live `client_id` and `secret` via the Dashboard.”

name: Devin
researcher_notes:
Devin (docs.devin.ai) – AI/research/media-native app as an MCP/agent toolkit: independent buildability verification

1) Auth method(s) the API uses
- Devin MCP server requires a Devin API key using the HTTP `Authorization` header:
  - Example config uses `Authorization: Bearer <API_KEY>` and `serverUrl: https://mcp.devin.ai/mcp`. Source: https://docs.devin.ai/work-with-devin/devin-mcp
- The Devin REST API authentication model is “principal + token” and credentials use the `cog_` prefix:
  - `Service User API Key` and `Personal Access Token (PAT)` are both stated as using `cog_`-prefixed tokens, sent in `Authorization: Bearer ...`. Source: https://docs.devin.ai/api-reference/authentication
- For the Devin MCP specifically, legacy API keys (`apk_` / `apk_user_`) are not supported; MCP requires a service user key (`cog_`):
  - “Legacy API keys (`apk_` / `apk_user_` prefix) are not supported by the Devin MCP server. Use a service user API key instead.” Source: https://docs.devin.ai/work-with-devin/devin-mcp
- Enterprise service-user keys / PATs require an additional `X-Org-Id` header to choose the org:
  - “Enterprise service user keys and PATs are scoped to the account level… you must tell the server which organization to target by passing the `X-Org-Id` header” Source: https://docs.devin.ai/work-with-devin/devin-mcp

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access (try to find signup / API key / pricing)

name: higgsfield
researcher_notes:
Higgsfield (“higgsfield.ai” + the `higgsfield` CLI) can be turned into an agent-toolkit in practice, and it already has a strong “agent surface” via an official MCP server + CLI “skills”.

Below is an evidence-backed buildability assessment across the 4 requested areas.

---

## 1) Auth method(s) the API uses

### API keys (REST-style underlying platform API via SDK)
Higgsfield’s official Python SDK documents that you authenticate by setting API credentials in environment variables, and supports either:
- a single combined key, or
- separate key + secret.  

From the Python client docs:
- `export HF_KEY="your-api-key:your-api-secret"`
- `export HF_API_KEY="your-api-key"`
- `export HF_API_SECRET="your-api-secret"`
and: “Get your credentials from the [Higgsfield Cloud]” (`cloud.higgsfield.ai`).  
Source: https://pypi.org/project/higgsfield-client/ (Auth section)  

Node SDK v2 docs additionally state the header format:
- `Authorization: Key KEY_ID:KEY_SECRET`  
Source: https://registry.npmjs.org/@higgsfield/client (section “V2 Client (Recommended)” → “Authentication”)

### OAuth-based auth for the official MCP server (no API keys in agent setup)
The official MCP server is hosted at `https://mcp.higgsfield.ai/mcp`, and the Higgsfield CLI/skills documentation says to authenticate via:
- `higgsfield auth login` (OAuth device-flow style described in repo docs)
and for MCP specifically the skills page says:
- “Do I need an API key? No. Add the Higgsfield MCP server URL … and authenticate through your Higgsfield account. No API keys to manage or configure.”

- `higgsfield auth login` command + OAuth mention (device-flow OAuth): https://github.com/higgsfield-ai/skills/blob/main/CLAUDE.md

name: Mermaid CLI
researcher_notes:
Mermaid CLI buildability as an AI-agent toolkit (MCP server / agent-callable actions)

1) Auth methods used by Mermaid CLI’s API surface
- Mermaid CLI (the open-source repo `mermaid-js/mermaid-cli`) does not describe an HTTP API with user authentication at all; it’s a local CLI that renders Mermaid text into files (SVG/PNG/PDF) and can be used via a local Node.js API. Evidence: the README says “It takes a mermaid definition file as input and generates an SVG/PNG/PDF file as output.” and shows only local usage patterns and Node API import calls.  
  URL: https://github.com/mermaid-js/mermaid-cli (README)
- The only credential/auth mechanism described in the Mermaid CLI repo is build-time access to GitHub’s package registry for `@mermaid-js` scoped packages, using a GitHub Personal Access Token placed in `.npmrc` (i.e., npm registry auth, not request-time auth for an API). Evidence phrases:
  - “The `@mermaid-js` scoped packages require authentication to GitHub's package registry.”
  - “Generate a Personal Access Token” and configure `.npmrc` with `//npm.pkg.github.com/:_authToken="YOUR-TOKEN"`.  
  URLs:
  - https://deepwiki.com/mermaid-js/mermaid-cli/5.1-local-setup
  - https://github.com/mermaid-js/mermaid-cli/blob/6e593e3b81aacaf7984151b96d7644daa58daa41/building.md

Conclusion for auth: there is no developer-facing runtime auth for an API because Mermaid CLI is local-only; auth appears only for installing private package registry dependencies during development/build. (No OAuth2/Bearer/JWT for a Mermaid CLI service is documented.)

- For running/rendering Mermaid CLI, no API keys are required. Evidence: README provides installation + local rendering usage without any token/auth steps.
